rulings.Case Title: DAVE JOLLY VS. PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Renewed Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Further Deposition Testimony
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, April 2, 2025, Line 3, DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS & ELECTRIC's Renewed Motion To Compel Plaintiffs Further Deposition Testimony. (Part 1 of 2). PG&E took five hours of plaintiff Dave Jolly's deposition testimony. When the deposition did not finish at the end of one business day, and the captioner whose participation was necessary was not available the next business day, PG&E stated its intent to keep the deposition open and Jolly's counsel stated his position that the deposition could continue the next day (under the terms of the notice that the deposition would occur "from day to day until complete") but that Jolly would not appear on a future date, even if the deposition were remote. (Cosmopulos Dec., Ex. E, p. 169-170.) PG&E noticed a continued deposition, and Jolly objected and refused to appear on a subsequent day. (Cosmopulos Dec. Exs. F, I.) PG&E now moves to compel what it deems the completion of the deposition and what Jolly deems another deposition in contravention of the one-deposition rule (CCP 2025.610(a).) The court grants the motion. The court agrees with Jolly that the deposition is untimely if brought as a motion to compel compliance with the first deposition notice pursuant to 2025.480(b); such a motion was due 60 days after the record was completed, on or about April 28, 2024. But PG&E served a second notice of deposition for a second day of testimony, timely filing its motion based on that notice, and alternatively argues there is good cause for more than one deposition of Jolly under 2025.610(b). The court agrees. The court cannot say under the circumstances here that it is so clear that PG&E should have been prepared with a captioner on the second day that its failure to do so amounts to forfeiture of its opportunity to complete its deposition of the plaintiff in this case. In any event, the limited time PG&E had with Plaintiff in the first session and the additional materials that have been subsequently produced warrant further testimony. The court orders Plaintiff to appear for a second day of deposition, on a date mutually agreed by the parties within 30 days of entry of this order. The deposition shall be remote unless PG&E pays travel costs for Jolly and his counsel. PG&E shall provide a captioner or similar accommodation. Both parties seek sanctions pursuant to 2025.480(j). Although Jolly opposed the motion, his position was warranted by a reasonable reading of 2025.480. The court finds substantial justification and does not award sanctions. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (Continued to Part 2).

rulings.Case Title: ADAM O'CONNOR VS. SCOTT NEWLIN JR. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration Or, In The Alternative, Motion For Relief From Admissions Pursuant To C.C.P Section 473
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, April 2, 2025, Line 4, PLAINTIFF ADAM O'CONNOR's Motion For Reconsideration Or, In The Alternative, Motion For Relief From Admissions Pursuant To C.C.P Section 473. (Part 1 of 2). Plaintiff Adam O'Connor seeks relief from the effect of his counsel's failure to oppose defendant American Medical Response West's motion for deemed admissions. On March 3, 2025, the court granted AMRW's motion for deemed admissions. O'Connor did not oppose the motion or contest the court's tentative ruling. If he had, he could have informed the court that fact discovery had closed on February 18, or that he had served responses to the requests for admission the day before, on Sunday evening before the motion was heard. (Labat Dec., Ex. D.) O'Connor now moves for reconsideration or relief under CCP 473. His counsel pleads a mistaken belief that there was no need to oppose the motion. The court denies relief under CCP 1008. No different facts exist that did not exist at the time of the hearing. O'Connor simply failed to participate in the hearing or to bring those facts to the court's attention. Respecting O'Connor's alternative request for relief under CCP 473(b), O'Connor has proceeded under the wrong statute. "The law governing the consequences for failing to respond to requests for admission may be the most unforgiving in civil procedure. There is no relief under section 473. The defaulting party is limited to the remedies available in the statute specifically governing requests for admission . . . ." (Demyer v. Costa Mesa Mobile Home Estates (1995) 36 Cal.App.4th 393, 394-395 [disapproved on other grounds by Wilcox v. Birtwhistle (1999) 21 Cal.4th 973].) That statute allows relief from admissions, including deemed admissions, pursuant to CCP 2033.300, which states: "(a) A party may withdraw or amend an admission made in response to a request for admission only on leave of court granted after notice to all parties. (b) The court may permit withdrawal or amendment of an admission only if it determines that the admission was the result of mistake, inadvertence, or excusable neglect, and that the party who obtained the admission will not be substantially prejudiced in maintaining that party's action or defense on the merits. (c) The court may impose conditions on the granting of the motion that are just, including, but not limited to, the following: (1) An order that the party who obtained the admission be permitted to pursue additional discovery related to the matter involved in the withdrawn or amended admission. (2) An order that the costs of any additional discovery be borne in whole or in part by the party withdrawing or amending the admission." Defendant AMRW opposes relief but has briefed the issue of 2033.300. The court exercises its discretion to treat the motion as if it sought relief under 2033.300. There is a liberal policy of deciding issues on their merits. O'Connor's counsel's error, if the court does not permit its correction, would amount to summary judgment in Defendant's favor and could spawn additional litigation. The court finds excusable neglect and grants relief conditionally pursuant to CCP 2033.300(c) as follows: The court orders discovery sanctions of $2000 payable by O'Connor's counsel to defendant AMRW. O'Connor is relieved of the deemed admissions on condition that this sanction is fully paid and on condition that any outstanding discovery sanctions in this case that are due from O'Connor or his counsel have been paid. Upon that showing to the trial court, the deemed admissions are withdrawn and the responses in Exhibit D to the Labat declaration will be effective. (Continued to Part 2).

rulings.Case Title: NKECHI EMERUWA-NEUBERG VS. FRANK LUCCHETTI ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Deposition
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, April 2, 2025, Line 6, DEFENDANT FRANK LUCCHETTI's Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Deposition. The court grants defendant Frank Lucchetti's motion to compel plaintiff Nkechi Emeruwa-Neuberg's deposition. There is good cause for further deposition of Plaintiff. The fact that Defendant has not yet submitted to deposition may be grounds for Plaintiff to seek separate relief in a motion from this court but is not grounds to oppose this motion. The deposition shall occur within 10 court days of entry of this order unless the parties agree to a different date. If the parties are unable to mutually agree on a date after reasonable efforts, defendant is authorized to set the date unilaterally. Lucchetti further seeks sanctions. The court seeks from the correspondence that counsel from both parties appear to have conditioned their client's availability for deposition on the production of the other party for deposition first. (See Corman Dec., Ex. E, Corman email to Drake et al dated July 31, 2024 and June 18, 2024.) The court finds both parties acted without substantial justification in that regard and does not award sanctions. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. =(301/CVA).

rulings.Case Title: JANE DOE VS. GAVIN NEWSOM ET AL
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion/Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, March 26, 2025, line 2, 2 - DEFENDANT PLUMPJACK MANAGEMENT GROUP LLC, BALBOA CAFE PARTNERS, HILARY NEWSOM, GAVIN NEWSOM, JEREMY SCHERER, DEANN GRAFFIGNA, GUILLAUME ISSAVERDEN MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT Defendants move to strike portions of the complaint pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 436, which permits the court to strike from a pleading any "irrelevant, false, or improper matter." (CCP 436(a).) The portions of the complaint defendants seek to strike concern Gavin Newsom and his political statements and public positions. By companion order, the court has sustained Gavin Newsom's demurrer to all causes of action without leave to amend. Accordingly, there is no continuing relevance to the Complaint's statements about him. The court grants the motion to strike the paragraphs and lines specified in Defendants' moving papers. The order prepared by the moving party should incorporate the specific paragraphs and lines from the notice of motion. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom.
